title: Huawei's Chip End-Run, Taiwan's Export Ban Debate, and the Underground AI Chip Trade
subhead: Huawei claims a path to 1.4nm-equivalent chips by 2031 without EUV; Taiwan considers criminal bans on AI chip exports to all of China; Chinese entities continue acquiring banned Nvidia silicon through gray markets.

sections.heading: Huawei Claims Chip Design Breakthrough to Bypass Sanctions
sections.body:
Huawei Technologies announced a new chip design methodology it claims can achieve transistor density equivalent to 1.4-nanometer processes by 2031, without relying on the extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography equipment blocked by US export controls. At the International Symposium of Circuits and Systems in Shanghai, Huawei introduced what it calls the 'Tau Scaling Law' and 'LogicFolding' architecture — an approach that vertically stacks chip layers rather than relying solely on shrinking transistors, according to reports from GSMArena, IBTimes, Reuters, and CNBC.

The company stated it has already mass-produced 381 chips using this methodology and that its next-generation Kirin smartphone processors, launching this fall, will adopt LogicFolding. Huawei framed the approach as an alternative to Moore's Law, which the company argues is hitting physical and economic limits. The announcement comes amid intensified US-China technology competition, with Washington tightening export controls on chip design software, manufacturing tools, and advanced semiconductor equipment since 2019.

Industry analysts note that while architectural innovation may improve efficiency, manufacturing constraints remain significant — thermal management, system integration, and production scalability continue to challenge efforts to match leading-edge global fabrication. China's most advanced domestic manufacturing capability is currently estimated at around the 7-nanometer level, according to Reuters. TSMC has said it expects to begin 1.4-nanometer production in the coming years.
sections.whyItMatters: If Huawei's claims prove viable, they represent a potential pathway for China to narrow the semiconductor gap without access to the most advanced manufacturing equipment — though the gap between design capability and mass production remains substantial.

sections.heading: Taiwan Weighs Criminal Ban on AI Chip Exports to China
sections.body:
Taiwan is considering stricter export controls that would criminalize AI chip sales to all customers in China, not just blacklisted firms, according to Bloomberg and MSN. The proposed measures would go beyond current US restrictions and could significantly reshape supply chains for advanced semiconductors.

Taiwanese authorities are weighing the tighter controls to align more closely with US export restrictions and address semiconductor smuggling risks. The move would mark a significant escalation from targeting specific entities to a blanket prohibition. Shares of TSMC slipped about 1% in premarket trading following the Bloomberg report, reflecting investor concerns about potential revenue impact from the world's largest contract chipmaker.

The proposal comes as TSMC chairman C.C. Wei said the company is 'not afraid' of competition from mainland Chinese foundries, dismissing concerns about advances by Huawei and Chinese chipmakers. Speaking at TSMC's annual shareholders' meeting, Wei said competition has been constant throughout the company's four-decade history, according to South China Morning Post. He also sought to reassure investors about TSMC's 16-nanometer fabrication plant in Nanjing, saying the facility faces no asset risk and enjoys strong support from local authorities.
sections.whyItMatters: Taiwan's potential shift from entity-based to country-wide restrictions would tighten the noose on China's AI chip access — but also puts Taiwan's foundry business model under strain as major customers face potential supply disruptions.

sections.heading: China's Military and Government Still Acquiring Banned Nvidia Chips
sections.body:
Chinese military bodies, state-run AI research institutes, and universities have continued purchasing small batches of Nvidia semiconductors banned by US export controls, according to a Reuters review of tender documents published by the Asahi Shimbun. The purchases include A100 and H100 chips banned since September 2022, as well as the slower A800 and H800 variants that Nvidia developed for China before those too were banned in October 2025.

The review found more than 100 tenders where state entities have procured A100 chips, with Tsinghua University alone purchasing approximately 80 A100 chips since the 2022 ban. Military purchasers include an unnamed People's Liberation Army entity in Wuxi, Jiangsu province, which sought three A100 chips in October and one H100 chip this month. The purchasers include institutions subject to US export restrictions, including Harbin Institute of Technology and the University of Electronic Science and Technology of China.

Neither Nvidia nor approved retailers were identified among the suppliers. The transactions occurred through an underground market that has emerged since the bans, with vendors sourcing excess stock from large US firms or importing through companies in India, Taiwan, and Singapore. Chris Miller, author of 'Chip War,' noted that making export restrictions watertight is unrealistic given chips are small and easily smuggled, but the goal is to 'throw sand in the gears of China's AI development' by making large clusters of advanced chips harder to build.
sections.whyItMatters: Despite export controls, banned chips continue flowing to Chinese entities through gray markets — but in quantities insufficient for training frontier AI models from scratch, suggesting the restrictions create friction rather than an absolute barrier.

sections.heading: FCC Proposes Undersea Cable Rules Targeting China Risk
sections.body:
The US Federal Communications Commission has proposed stricter oversight of submarine communications cables that handle 99% of international internet traffic, with rules designed to make it easier for trusted US tech companies like Meta and Google to expand global cable networks while tightening restrictions on Chinese-linked equipment, according to Yahoo Finance and Reuters.

The draft rules under consideration would streamline deployment for trusted operators while enhancing security against foreign threats from China. The move reflects growing concerns about the security of undersea infrastructure that carries critical internet traffic and data flows between continents.

The proposed rules come as undersea cable infrastructure faces heightened geopolitical risk. In the Gulf region, countries like Saudi Arabia and the UAE have invested billions in AI infrastructure but remain dependent on a narrow concentration of cable routes through the Red Sea and Strait of Hormuz. Following US-Iran-Israel tensions earlier this year, media reports suggested Iran considered targeting all seven undersea cables running through the Strait of Hormuz, according to WIRED Middle East. In 2025, two cables linking Europe to the Middle East and Asia were cut in the Red Sea, causing an estimated $3.5 billion in damages from lost services.

Gulf countries are now pursuing multilayered connectivity strategies, including terrestrial corridors through Saudi Arabia, Jordan, and Turkey, and projects like Saudi Arabia's $800 million SilkLink investment and the $700 million WorldLink cable through Iraq, aiming to reduce dependence on maritime chokepoints.
sections.whyItMatters: Undersea cables are becoming strategic infrastructure as AI-driven data flows increase — and the FCC's rules signal that Washington wants trusted American firms to control more of that infrastructure while locking out Chinese competitors.

sections.heading: SpaceX Reveals AI-Focused Satellite Before IPO
sections.body:
SpaceX unveiled details about its first 'AI satellite' ahead of the company's anticipated IPO on Friday, according to 24/7 Wall St. The spacecraft features a 70-meter wingspan — nearly three-quarters of a football field — with up to 150 kilowatts of computing capacity and 110-square-meter thermal radiators designed to dump heat into space.

Unlike Starlink satellites, which are primarily communications platforms, the AI1 satellite is designed as an orbital computing platform first, with most of its structure dedicated to solar arrays, AI chips, and cooling systems rather than communications hardware. SpaceX CEO Elon Musk described the satellite as simpler than Starlink because it eliminates much of the complex communications hardware while dramatically increasing power generation and cooling requirements.

The announcement reflects SpaceX's broader strategy to position itself beyond launch services and broadband connectivity. Space-based AI infrastructure could address terrestrial data center constraints — electricity shortages, power transmission bottlenecks, land availability, and water consumption — by leveraging constant solar energy in orbit. However, challenges remain: launch costs, component radiation exposure, and the need to demonstrate clear economic advantages over rapidly improving terrestrial infrastructure.
sections.whyItMatters: SpaceX is signaling that its long-term valuation thesis extends beyond Starlink — and that orbital AI compute could become a trillion-dollar market, though the economics remain unproven.

sections.heading: Critical Minerals Diplomacy Surges, But Most Deals Lack Teeth
sections.body:
Governments have signed more than 70 critical minerals agreements and policy frameworks since 2021, with over 50 bilateral and multilateral agreements in the past 18 months alone, according to Mining.com. Yet a review shows that more than 60% remain non-binding memorandums, partnerships, or cooperation frameworks without specific commitments tied to investments, production targets, or financing mechanisms.

The US illustrates the gap: Washington has signed more than 20 agreements in 18 months but only a handful are legally binding. China continues to dominate mineral processing, controlling an estimated 60% to 90% of global rare-earth refining capacity. Meanwhile, the US has sought to deepen ties with resource-rich jurisdictions like the Democratic Republic of Congo, while Zambia rejected proposed US conditions for a minerals agreement, signaling producer nations are asserting greater control over terms.

In the US, bipartisan legislation introduced by Representative John Moolenaar (R-MI) and Representative Ro Khanna (D-CA) aims to strengthen domestic magnet supply chains with production tax credits. The Magnets Value Chain Support Act of 2026 targets rare earth permanent magnets used in drone motors, electric vehicles, and defense systems — areas where China produces more than 90% of global supply, according to DRONELIFE. The bill would provide credits for rare earth processing, magnet manufacturing, and motor manufacturers purchasing US-produced magnets.
sections.whyItMatters: The critical minerals race is real, but the legal and financial foundations remain underdeveloped — binding agreements with specific commitments are concentrated among a small group of strategic partners like Australia, Japan, Argentina, Ukraine, and the DRC.

sections.heading: Iranian Hackers Use Ransomware as Decoy for Cyber-Espionage
sections.body:
The Iranian state-sponsored hacking group MuddyWater is using Chaos ransomware as a decoy to conceal cyber-espionage operations, according to Rapid7 research reported by BleepingComputer. The attacks involve Microsoft Teams social engineering, where attackers initiate chats with employees, establish screen-sharing sessions, harvest credentials, manipulate multi-factor authentication settings, and deploy remote access tools like AnyDesk.

Rapid7 attributed the campaign to MuddyWater — also known as Static Kitten, Mango Sandstorm, and Seedworm — based on infrastructure overlap and a code-signing certificate used to sign malware attributed to the group. The threat actor is believed to align with Iran's Ministry of Intelligence and Security and has previously used ransomware to mask espionage operations, including deploying Qilin ransomware against an Israeli organization in late 2025.

The attack progression shows credential theft through phishing pages disguised as Microsoft Quick Assist, persistence establishment via RDP and AnyDesk, and deployment of a custom backdoor with anti-analysis checks supporting 12 commands including PowerShell execution and file operations. The ransomware component was likely used to complicate attribution and disguise the primary espionage goal.
sections.whyItMatters: The campaign highlights convergence between state-sponsored intrusion activity and criminal tradecraft — the tell lies in which techniques were deployed and which weren't, suggesting the primary goal was not financial gain.

bottomLine: Today's developments reveal the cat-and-mouse nature of tech geopolitics: Huawei claims a sanctions-proof chip design path, but Taiwan considers making AI chip exports to China criminally illegal. Chinese entities keep acquiring banned Nvidia silicon through gray markets — in quantities that create friction but insufficient for frontier AI training. The FCC moves to lock Chinese firms out of undersea cable infrastructure while the Gulf scrambles to diversify connectivity routes. Critical minerals agreements proliferate but mostly lack enforcement mechanisms. And Iran's intelligence apparatus uses ransomware as camouflage for espionage operations.
sources: GSMArena, IBTimes, Reuters, CNBC, Bloomberg, MSN, South China Morning Post, Asahi Shimbun, Yahoo Finance, WIRED Middle East, 24/7 Wall St., Mining.com, DRONELIFE, BleepingComputer, Rapid7
